name: Ladino (Judeo-Spanish)

ancestors.notes: Medieval Castilian (pre-1492 expulsion). The Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain in 1492 carried 15th-century Castilian with them; the language developed in diaspora (Ottoman Empire, North Africa, Balkans) with no further contact with evolving Castilian.

notes: Ladino (Judezmo / Judeo-Spanish / Spanyolit). Romance language descending from medieval Castilian, preserved since the 1492 expulsion of Jews from Spain. Spoken by Sephardic communities in Turkey (Istanbul, Izmir), Greece (Thessaloniki), Israel, Morocco, and elsewhere. ~60,000–100,000 speakers (UNESCO: seriously endangered). Written in Rashi/Solitreo script historically; modern use favors Latin alphabet (Autoridad Nasionala del Ladino standard, Israel). KEY PHONOLOGICAL FEATURES: (1) SIBILANT PRESERVATION: /ʃ/ (⟨x/š⟩) and /ʒ/ (⟨j/zh⟩) are MAINTAINED — they never merged to /x/ as in modern Spanish. This is the most diagnostic feature. (2) AFFRICATES: /ts/ (⟨ts/ç⟩) and /dʒ/ (⟨dj⟩) preserved from medieval Castilian. (3) SESEO: no /θ/ — Latin c/z both → /s/ (like Andalusian/American Spanish). (4) BETACISM: /b/ and /v/ fully merged (⟨v⟩=⟨b⟩=[b]/[β]). (5) PALATAL LATERAL: /ʎ/ (⟨ll⟩) preserved in conservative varieties (unlike modern Spanish yeísmo). (6) F-PRESERVATION: initial F- maintained as [f] (Spanish underwent F→h→∅ but Ladino kept [f]: hijo vs Ladino fijo). (7) NO /x/: the modern Spanish velar fricative does not exist in Ladino. (8) VOWELS: 5-vowel system, some e~ɛ and o~ɔ alternation. (9) LOANWORDS: extensive Ottoman Turkish, Greek, Hebrew, and French borrowings.
